Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


000
001
      Anomaly Detection by Estimating Gradients of the
002   Tabular Data Distribution
003
004
005       Anonymous authors
006       Paper under double-blind review
007
008
009                                                 Abstract
010
011              Detecting anomalies in tabular data from various domains has become increasingly
012              important in deep learning research. Simultaneously, the development of genera-
013              tive models has advanced, offering powerful mechanisms for detecting anomalies
014              by modeling normal data. In this paper, we propose a novel method for anomaly
015              detection in a one-class classification setting using a noise conditional score net-
016              work (NCSN). NCSNs, which can learn the gradients of log probability density
017              functions over many noise-perturbed data distributions, are known for their diverse
018
                 sampling even in low-density regions of the training data. This effect can also be
                 utilized, and thus, the NCSN can be used directly as an anomaly indicator with an
019
                 anomaly score derived from a simplified loss function. This effect will be analyzed
020
                 in detail. Our method is trained on normal behavior data, enabling it to differ-
021              entiate between normal and anomalous behaviors in test scenarios. To evaluate
022              our approach extensively, we created the world’s largest benchmark for anomaly
023              detection in tabular data with 49 baseline methods consisting of the ADBench
024              benchmark and several more datasets from the literature. Overall, our approach
025              shows state-of-the-art performance across the benchmark.
026
027
028   1     Introduction
029
030   Anomaly detection, the process of identifying data points that deviate significantly from expected
031   patterns, is a critical task with applications spanning industries such as finance (Al-Hashedi &
032   Magalingam, 2021), healthcare (Fernando et al., 2021), manufacturing (Gupta et al., 2013), and
033
      cybersecurity (Ahmad et al., 2021). While traditional machine learning techniques such as nearest
      neighbors and density-based methods have historically dominated the anomaly detection landscape,
034
      particularly in tabular data, their performance has shown limitations in high-dimensional, large-scale
035
      datasets. This is especially true as data complexity increases and the demands for scalability and
036   real-time processing become more pressing. Despite advances, traditional methods often struggle
037   to cope with the intricate patterns present in modern data, making them less suitable for complex
038   applications (Ramaswamy et al., 2000).
039
      The increasing availability of high-dimensional data has fueled research into more sophisticated
040
      methods, particularly generative models, which have emerged as powerful tools for capturing the
041
      underlying distributions of normal data. By learning the data manifold of normal behavior, generative
042   models provide a promising mechanism for anomaly detection by modeling deviations from this
043   manifold as potential anomalies. Among these approaches, score-based generative models, such as
044   Noise Conditional Score Networks (NCSN) (Song & Ermon, 2019), have gained significant attention
045   for their ability to learn the gradient of the log-likelihood functions across noisy data distributions.
046   Unlike traditional generative models like GANs and VAEs, which often face challenges in computing
047   exact probabilities, NCSNs excel at this task, making them a natural fit for detecting anomalies
048   (Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021). NCSN are also closely related to Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic
049   Models (DDPM) (Karras et al., 2022).
050   The anomaly detection problem is often framed as a one-class classification task, where the objective
051   is to learn a model that characterizes normal behavior from training data and can distinguish between
052   normal and anomalous observations during testing (Schölkopf et al., 1999). This is also often called
053   semi-supervised learning (Livernoche et al., 2024). Unlike traditional supervised learning tasks,
      where labeled data is available for all classes, anomaly detection typically lacks labeled examples of


                                                          1
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


054
      anomalous data. This makes the task more difficult, as the model must generalize from normal data
055   alone and detect subtle deviations that indicate an anomaly. All the designations have in common
056   that the training is unsupervised, i.e., without labels, and the preselection only takes place in data
057   preprocessing.
058
      In this paper, we propose a novel approach to anomaly detection in a Learning from Positive
059
      Unlabelled Examples (LPUE) or one-class classification setting that leverages the inherent strengths
060
      of score-based models. Our method introduces an NCSN-based diffusion network trained on normal
061   data, which learns in the training to differentiate between normal and anomalous behavior during
062   testing. The essential advantage of this approach lies in the ability of NCSNs to discriminate
063   perturbed samples on the data manifold through a score-based error. The score variation between
064   normal and anomalous samples provides a robust mechanism for anomaly detection. A well-trained
065   network enables the output of a direct score on which a sample can be assessed as an anomaly or
066   not, making this approach both intuitive and effective.
067   Our motivation for employing score-based models is rooted in the limitations of existing approaches.
068   Traditional methods, such as reconstruction-based techniques, rely on the assumption that anomalies
069   produce a higher reconstruction error. Still, these approaches often require external knowledge
070   (Tack et al., 2020) or pre-trained models (Fort et al., 2021), which limits their transferability and
071   scalability. Similarly, while embedding-based and synthetic anomaly-based methods have shown
072   strong performance on industrial benchmarks, they depend on large datasets and leverage external
073   knowledge, which may not always be available or transferable. In contrast, score-based models,
074   particularly NCSNs, offer a self-contained and mathematically sound approach to anomaly detection
075
      that does not rely on external priors or complex architectures (Shin et al., 2023).
076   To demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, we conduct extensive experiments on the largest
077   benchmark for tabular anomaly detection, ADBench benchmark (Han et al., 2022), which includes
078   57 datasets spanning diverse domains such as finance, healthcare, and industrial applications. This
079   benchmark contains 47 tabular datasets, as well as datasets consisting of extracted representations
080   from images and of extracted embeddings of natural language tasks, with a total of 121 sub-datasets,
081
      providing a comprehensive evaluation of our method across different modalities. Moreover, we
      add 15 further frequently used datasets from other studies. In this new benchmark, we compare
082
      our approach against 49 baseline methods, including both classical and state-of-the-art techniques,
083
      and we show that our NCSN-based model, called noise conditional score-based anomaly detection
084   (NCSBAD), outperforms existing methods in terms of detection accuracy and scalability.
085
086   Our key contributions are summarized as follows:
087
             • We present a novel approach for one-class anomaly detection utilizing a score-based model
088
               with a simplified loss function, which operates without needing external knowledge such as
089            external priors, pre-trained models or additional datasets.
090
091
             • We perform the first empirical study of NCSN anomaly detection on tabular data, where
               our adaptation approach shows a high performance and interpretability.
092
093          • We demonstrate the inherent capability of the trained score model to effectively identify
094            anomalies, and we provide a thorough analysis of how various parameters, including the
095
               network architecture, impact its performance.
096          • Through comprehensive experiments on well-established public benchmarks, including
097            ADBench and other widely used tabular datasets, we demonstrate that our approach consis-
098            tently achieves state-of-the-art results in tabular anomaly detection, outperforming existing
099            methods across multiple metrics.
100
101   2   Background
102
103   In anomaly detection, the unsupervised setting is particularly challenging because anomalies typically
104   constitute only a small fraction of the overall data, and labels are unavailable during training. This
105   scenario assumes that the data consists of a mixture of normal and anomalous samples without access
106   to label information. Many methods in this category rely on assumptions about the underlying data-
107   generation process, making techniques such as embedding models and deep generative models
      well-suited candidates. However, deep models often struggle with the tendency to inadvertently


                                                        2
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


108
      model the anomalies within the input data, thus complicating distinguishing them. In contrast, one-
109   class classification approaches within semi-supervised anomaly detection are specifically designed
110   to address this issue (Ruff et al., 2021). This idea relies on LPUE, where anomaly detectors are
111   trained on positive data only and then validated/tested on both normal and abnormal data (Golan &
112   El-Yaniv, 2018; Sabokrou et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020).
113
      In this work, we focus on detecting anomalies without prior knowledge of their distribution, thereby
114
      avoiding reliance on labeled data for training. Various unsupervised methods aim to estimate the
115   density of the data distribution 𝑝(x) or its log-likelihood log 𝑝(x). Score matching, introduced
116   by Hyvärinen & Dayan (2005), provides a way to estimate data density by circumventing the
117   intractable computation of partition functions in parametric models. Vincent (2011) established a
118   critical connection between the denoising auto-encoder and score matching, demonstrating that the
119   denoising auto-encoder objective is equivalent to the score-matching objective. Building on this
120   principle, we adopt the denoising score-matching approach to learn an approximation of the score
121   introduced by Song & Ermon (2019), which forms the core of our method for anomaly detection.
122   The score of a probability density function 𝑝(x), is defined as ∇x log 𝑝(x). Score-based generative
123   models estimate this score, ∇x log 𝑝(x). Unlike likelihood-based models, score-based models do
124   not require normalization and simplifying parameterization. A non-normalized statistical model has
                          −𝐸 ( x)
125   the form 𝑝 𝜃 (x) = 𝑒 𝑍𝜃𝜃 , where 𝐸 𝜃 (x) ∈ R is known as the energy function, and 𝑍 𝜃 is an unknown
126   normalizing constant that ensures 𝑝 𝜃 (x) constitutes a valid probability density. In traditional
127   likelihood-based training, calculating the normalizing constant 𝑍 𝜃 is intractable. Focusing on the
128   score ∇x log 𝑝 𝜃 (x) = −∇x 𝐸 𝜃 (x), eliminates the need to compute the normalizing constant 𝑍 𝜃 .
129
      While diffusion models can be represented through various mathematical formulations, either discrete
130
      or continuous, (Song et al., 2020b) offers a unified framework using Stochastic Differential Equations
131   (SDEs). In this framework, the forward diffusion process is defined as follows: Let {x𝑡 ∈ R𝑑 }𝑇𝑡=0
132   represent a diffusion process indexed by the continuous time variable 𝑡 ∈ [0, 𝑇]. This diffusion
133   process is governed by an SDE, given by
134
135
                                             𝑑x = f (x, 𝑡)𝑑𝑡 + 𝑔(𝑡)𝑑w𝑡 ,                                       (1)
136   where f (·, 𝑡) : R𝑑   → R𝑑 is the drift coefficient, 𝑔(𝑡)      ∈ R is the diffusion coefficient, and w𝑡
137   represents Brownian motion or Wiener process.
138
      The optimization problem is formulated as a weighted sum of Fisher divergences for all noise scales:
139
140           min E𝑡∼U (0,𝑇 ) 𝜆(𝑡)Ex0 ∼ 𝑝 ( x0 ) Ex𝑡 ∼ 𝑝 ( x𝑡 | x0 ) ∥𝑆 𝜃 (x𝑡 , 𝑡) − ∇x𝑡 log 𝑝(x𝑡 | x0 ) ∥ 22 , (2)
                 𝜃
141
142   where U (0, 𝑇) denotes the uniform distribution over the interval [0, 𝑇], and 𝑝(x𝑡 | x0 ) represents
143
      the transition probability from x0 to x𝑡 . The weighting function 𝜆(𝑡), is a positive, time-dependent
      function, 𝜆(𝑡) ∈ R>0 . In this objective, the expectation over the initial data distribution 𝑝 0 can be
144
      efficiently estimated by computing empirical means over observed data samples. The expectation
145
      over x𝑡 can be estimated by sampling from the transition probability 𝑝(x𝑡 | x0 ) (Song & Ermon,
146   2019; 2020; Song et al., 2020b).
147
148
149   3   Method
150
151   Figure 1 provides a general overview of our approach. In this section, we first define the tabular data
152
      anomaly detection task and then describe the design of our method in detail. The algorithms for
      training and inference are included in Appendix B.
153
154   Problem Statement Given a dataset of 𝑁 normal behavior samples S = {x1 , ..., x 𝑁 }, where each
155   sample x𝑛 is a vector in R𝑑 , our goal is to develop a scoring function 𝑦 : R𝑑 → R. This function
156   should assign lower scores to samples drawn from the same distribution as S, while higher scores
157   are assigned to samples that deviate from this distribution. This enables the distinction between
158
      in-distribution and out-of-distribution samples. For interpretability purposes, we aim to ensure that
      the scoring function 𝑦 𝑖 : R𝑑 → R𝑑 can also provide direct, feature-wise access to the results.
159
160   Score Network for Tabular Anomaly Detection We modify the denoising score matching tech-
161   nique specifically for tabular data and introduce a noise-conditional score network that learns patterns
      from the training data. It maps samples from the target distribution 𝑝(x0 ) to a prior distribution


                                                           3
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


162
163
164
165
166
167
168
169
170                  (a) Training Principle                                 (b) Inference Principle
171
172   Figure 1: Overview of the proposed method. Each input data point x is transformed through a
173   diffusion process from x0 to x𝑇 . During the training phase, the model learns a vector field (the
174   score) that characterizes the underlying data distribution (shown on the right). In the inference
175   phase, the trained model evaluates each sample, assigning an anomaly score based on its likelihood
176
      of residing within the learned vector field.
177
178
      𝑝(x𝑇 ). To approximate the time-dependent score function ∇x𝑡 log 𝑝(x𝑡 ), we train a score-based
179
      model 𝑆 𝜃 (x𝑡 , 𝑡) using a simplified denoising score matching. The parameters of the score network
180   are trained by minimizing a simplified loss function where we directly predict the noise ε,
181
182                          ℓ(𝜃) = E𝑡∼U (0,𝑇 ) Ex0 ∼ 𝑝 ( x0 ) Eε∼N (0,I ) ∥𝑆 𝜃 (x𝑡 , 𝑡) − ε∥ 22 ,    (3)
183
      where 𝑡 ∼ U (0, 𝑇) denotes the random drawn timestep, x𝑡 = x0 + 𝜎𝑡 ε the perturbed sample at
184
      timestep 𝑡 and ε ∼ N (0, I) denotes the Gaussian noise. The derivation is outlined in Appendix C.
185   Then 𝑆 𝜃 (x𝑡 , 𝑡) is a neural network that learns the denoising function to approximate the Gaussian
186   noise using the perturbed data x𝑡 and the time 𝑡. We use the standard 𝐿 2 loss for the denoising score
187   matching, without adding any of the in Section 2 described weighting adjustments.
188
      In general, every neural network that maps an input vector x ∈ R𝑑 to an output vector y ∈ R𝑑 with
189
      the same input and output dimensions can be used as a score-based model. This framework offers
190
      significant flexibility in choosing model architectures. To design our conditional score network,
191   we use a simple multi-layer perceptron (MLP), a type of neural networks. While image-based
192   approaches often use U-Net architectures, MLPs are more commonly used for tabular data, as shown
193   in Section 6 and in the baseline methods. Our network architecture study, detailed in Appendix A,
194   confirms that MLPs are a good fit for this task, working well with the datasets we evaluated.
195
      Network Architecture The MLP, hereafter referred to as MLP2048, approximates the de-
196
      noising function ε 𝜃 (𝑥 𝑡 , 𝑡) and consists of three hidden layers, each using SiLU activation func-
197   tions. Time-step embeddings, represented as 𝑡𝑖𝑚𝑒 emb , are created using Gaussian random features
198   [sin(2𝜋𝜔𝑡); cos(2𝜋𝜔𝑡)] (Tancik et al., 2020). These embeddings enable the network to capture the
199   time dimension.
200
      The MLP2048 takes two inputs: the current time step 𝑡 and the input vector x𝑡 ∈ R1×𝑑𝑖𝑛 , with
201
      𝑥 𝑡 = x0 + 𝜎𝑡 ε. First, 𝑥 𝑡 is passed through a linear projection layer, which adjusts its dimension to
202
      𝑑hidden , with ℎ0 = 𝐹𝐶in (𝑥 𝑡 ) ∈ R1×𝑑hidden . Following the TabDDPM approach Kotelnikov et al. (2023),
203   we add the time-step embeddings 𝑡𝑖𝑚𝑒 emb ∈ R1×𝑑hidden to ℎ0 to create the input for the hidden layers
204   ℎhidden1 = ℎ0 + 𝑡𝑖𝑚𝑒 emb .
205
206
      The network has three hidden layers that process the input, and the final output score is calculated
      by the last linear layer ε 𝜃 (x𝑡 , 𝑡) = ℎout = 𝐹𝐶out (ℎ3 ) ∈ R1×𝑑in . Specifically, the hidden layers of the
207
      MLP2048 are fully connected, and the dimension is doubled in the first hidden layer and halved in
208
      the last hidden layer to ensure the network can learn detailed patterns.
209
210   To avoid a reduction of the input dimensionality and, therefore, a compression after the input layer,
211   we use a large hidden dimension of 2048, which is bigger than the maximum number of features in
212
      our datasets. This helps capture more information. More details about the network’s architecture,
      including ablation studies and parameter choices, can be found in Appendix A and E.
213
214   Noise Scale Selection The choice of noise scale is crucial in score estimation, as it determines
215   how well low-density regions of the data distribution are captured. Therefore, it is the most crucial
      hyperparameter in our method. While larger noise scales can effectively cover these regions, they


                                                           4
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


216
      tend to over-corrupt the data, distorting it significantly from the original distribution. On the other
217   hand, smaller noise scales preserve the data’s structure but fail to adequately represent the low-density
218   regions.
219
220
      To strike a balance between these two extremes, we adopt a multi-scale noise perturbation approach
      inspired by previous work (Song & Ermon, 2020). Specifically, we perturb the data using isotropic
221
      Gaussian noise at timestep 𝑡 increasing standard deviations, 𝜎1 < 𝜎2 < · · · < 𝜎𝑇 , where each 𝜎𝑡
222
      introduces a different level of perturbation. The data distribution 𝑝(x0 )∫ is perturbed with Gaussian
223
      noise N (0, 𝜎𝑡2 I), resulting in noise-perturbed distributions 𝑝(x𝑡 ) = 𝑝(y)N (x0 ; y, 𝜎𝑡2 I)𝑑y ≈
224
      N(x0 ; 0, 𝜎𝑡2 I).
225
226   Since we aim to model the original data distribution more accurately, especially for anomaly detection,
227   we prioritize learning from lower noise scales. We ensure minimal distortion while capturing enough
228   information from low-density regions by employing
                                                     √︁     a moderate maximum noise scale. Therefore we
229   define the multiscale variance 𝜎𝑡 with 𝜎𝑡 = (𝜎 − 1)/2 log 𝜎 and 𝑡 ∼ U (0, 𝑇) and set 𝜎 = 0.01.
                                       2                 2𝑡

230   This results in a maximum of 𝜎𝑡 of 𝜎𝑇 ≈ 0.5 and also a good approximation of low-density regions.
231   Our optimized NCSN 𝑆 𝜃 ∗ (x𝑡 , 𝑡) estimates ∇x log 𝑝 𝑡 (x), such that −𝑆 𝜃 ∗ (x𝑡 , 𝑡)/𝜎𝑡 ≈ ∇x log 𝑝 𝑡 (x)
232
      in our simplified special case.
233   Anomaly Score Formulation We propose a novel anomaly score that is based on the simplified
234   score-based loss function, which serves as a robust discriminative measure for detecting anomalies.
235   The anomaly score evaluates how likely a perturbed sample matches the same data distribution as
236   the training data. This provides an effective way to distinguish anomalous data points from the
237   in-distribution samples.
238   Let 𝑥 𝑡 𝑓 𝑖𝑥 = 𝑥 0 + 𝜎𝑡 𝑓 𝑖𝑥 ε represent the perturbed sample feed into the score-based model where
239   ε ∼ N (0, I). Here, the noise level 𝑡 𝑓 𝑖𝑥 is fixed during inference. To ensure that x𝑡 𝑓 𝑖 𝑥 is close
240   to x0 , the perturbations should be small. This ensures that the perturbation is not too large and
241   still sufficient for a neural network. To determine an appropriate fixed 𝑡 𝑓 𝑖𝑥 , we make the following
242   consideration, if we assume 1000 time steps for 𝑡, 𝑡 𝑓 𝑖 𝑥 is chosen so that it corresponds to the first
243   time step. The anomaly score for a sample x is defined as:
244

                                       𝐴𝐷𝑆 = Eε∼N (0,I ) ∥𝑆 𝜃 ∗ (x𝑡 𝑓 𝑖 𝑥 , 𝑡 𝑓 𝑖 𝑥 ) − ε∥ 22 ,           (4)
245
246   which corresponds to the summed error of each output neuron. Since the noise scale 𝑡 is fixed, the
247   only randomness comes from ε. To reduce the influence, we perform the calculation with 𝑁𝑈 𝑀 = 70
248
      randomly chosen values for ε.
249   In practical scenarios, especially for identifying anomalous regions within a sample x ∈ R𝑑 , we can
250   skip the summation and use the feature-wise error measure directly. This error, denoted by 𝐴𝐷𝑆𝑖 , is
251   computed as:
252                               𝐴𝐷𝑆𝑖 = Eε∼N (0,I ) ∥𝑆 𝜃 ∗ (x𝑡 𝑓 𝑖 𝑥 , 𝑡 𝑓 𝑖𝑥 )𝑖 − ε𝑖 ∥ 22 ,           (5)
253   where 𝑖 refers to individual features of the data, and 𝐴𝐷𝑆𝑖 captures the element-wise anomaly score
254   for feature 𝑖. This feature-wise error provides an intuitive and interpretable measure for detecting
255   localized anomalies, as demonstrated in a toy example in Section 5.
256
      Benefit of Validation Data Due to the stochastic nature of the NCSN training, it can significantly
257
      benefit from using a validation dataset that is never used in training or inference. In general we
258   train our NCSN for 200 Epochs however, since 𝑡 and ε are randomly drawn from the respective
259   distributions 𝑡 ∼ U (0, 𝑇) and ε ∼ N (0, I), it cannot be guaranteed that this corresponds to the
260   optimal training endpoint in terms of anomaly detection. Therefore, if a validation set is available as
261   described in Section 4, a better training point based on the AUC-ROC on the validation data may be
262   chosen and used for inference. This broadly corresponds to the idea of early stopping but is evaluated
263   over the entire training process without causing a termination. We call this version NCSBADVAL.
264   Efficient Implementation and Inference Since we predict ε directly, it is important to highlight
265   that the anomaly score proposed here differs from the score function used in Section 2 of the
266   paper for denoising. Our anomaly detection framework is summarized in Algorithms 1 and 2,
267   which outlines the steps for calculating the anomaly detection score. Despite some computational
268   adjustments, the process is amenable to parallelization, as it does not require sequential operations.
269   This is a significant advantage over DDPMs, whose calculations based on a Markov chain can only
      be performed sequentially and cannot be parallelized. This parallelism significantly accelerates


                                                         5
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


270
      inference, making the method suitable for real-time anomaly detection applications. Further studies
271   on the implementation can be found in Appendix A.
272
273
274   4    Experiments
275
276   Datasets To ensure optimal comparable results in our study, we use all datasets that were used in
277   the largest comparisons and parts of the code of previous works (Livernoche et al., 2024) 1, (Shenkar
278
      & Wolf, 2022) 2, (Bouman et al., 2024) 3, (Han et al., 2022) 4, (Zhao et al., 2019) 5. In addition to
      ADBench benchmark (Han et al., 2022), which includes a collection of widely-used tabular anomaly
279
      detection datasets as well as newly generated datasets derived from images and natural language
280
      tasks we include datasets from ODDS (Ray), ELKI (Schubert & Zimek, 2019), ex-AE (Shin & Kim,
281   2020), the comparison from Goldstein & Uchida (2016) and from ICL (Shenkar & Wolf, 2022). In
282   total, ADBench consists of 57 datasets with 121 sub-datasets, 47 from tabular datasets, five from
283   extracted image representations, five from extracted embeddings from NLP areas, and 15 additional
284   datasets. Please see Table 4 and the supplementary material for further information.
285
      Experimental Setup In accordance with the previous approaches (Bergman & Hoshen, 2020;
286
      Shenkar & Wolf, 2022; Livernoche et al., 2024), the training set is constructed by randomly selecting
287   50% of the normal samples. The remaining 50% of normal samples and the full set of anomalies
288   are divided into a test and a validation set at a ratio of 60% to 40%. In contrast to Livernoche
289   et al. (2024), this is done to be able to carry out the experiments with validation data and allows a
290   comparison between the choice of the optimal training time selected based on validation data never
291   used in training and testing and a fixed number of training epochs. Consistent with prior work
292   (Bergman & Hoshen, 2020), the decision threshold for the anomaly score is determined such that
293   the predicted number of anomalies aligns with the actual number of anomalies present in the data.
294   For each dataset, we apply a standardization based on the training samples.
295   Baseline Methods We compare our approach with a wide range of anomaly detection methods,
296   including classical and deep learning-based models. We evaluate against the unsupervised trainable
297   methods from PyOD (Zhao et al., 2019), ADBench benchmark, and from Bouman et al. (2024). The
298   classical approaches encompass CBLOF (He et al., 2003), INNE (Bandaragoda et al., 2018), KPCA
299   (Hoffmann, 2007), KDE (Latecki et al., 2007), GMM (Agarwal, 2007), SOD (Kriegel et al., 2009),
300   ALAD (Zenati et al., 2018), COPOD (Li et al., 2020), ECOD (Li et al., 2022), FeatureBagging
301
      (Lazarevic & Kumar, 2005), HBOS (Goldstein & Dengel, 2012), IForest (Liu et al., 2008), kNN
      (Ramaswamy et al., 2000), LODA (Pevnỳ, 2016), LOF (Breunig et al., 2000), MCD (Fauconnier &
302
      Haesbroeck, 2009), OCSVM (Schölkopf et al., 1999), PCA (Shyu et al., 2003), EIF (Hariri et al.,
303
      2019), Ensemble LOF (Breunig et al., 2000)(Bouman et al., 2024), GEN2OUT (Lee et al., 2021),
304   DynamicHOBS (Goldstein & Dengel, 2012; Bouman et al., 2024), COF (Tang et al., 2002), ABOD
305   (Kriegel et al., 2008), LMDD (Arning et al., 1996), ODIN (Hautamaki et al., 2004), CD (Cook,
306   1977), QMCD (Fang & Ma, 2001), and Sampling (Sugiyama & Borgwardt, 2013). Meanwhile,
307   the deep learning-based models include methods such as LUNAR (Goodge et al., 2022), SOGAAL
308   (Liu et al., 2019), AE (Aggarwal & Aggarwal, 2017; Ramaswamy et al., 2000), DeepSVDD (Ruff
309   et al., 2018), DAGMM (Zong et al., 2018), GANomaly (Akcay et al., 2019), and MOGAAL (Liu
310   et al., 2019). Additionally, we incorporate comparisons with other proposed deep learning methods
311   such as DROCC (Goyal et al., 2020), GOAD (Bergman & Hoshen, 2020), ICL (Shenkar & Wolf,
312
      2022), PlanarFlow (Rezende & Mohamed, 2015), VAE (Kingma, 2013; Zhou et al., 2020), SLAD
      (Xu et al., 2023b), and DIF (Xu et al., 2023a) from Livernoche et al. (2024). Furthermore, we
313
      benchmark our approach against the latest techniques including DDPM, DTE-IG, DTE-C, and DTE-
314
      NP, also proposed by Livernoche et al. (2024), as well as 3WNCROD (Zhang et al., 2023b) and
315   MCM (Yin et al., 2024). To ensure the scope of our comparisons is robust, we include a dedicated
316   evaluation of a Transformer-based model and NTP-AD proposed by Thimonier et al. (2023), which
317   is detailed in Appendix D. These models demand specialized hyperparameter tuning for each dataset,
318   making a direct comparison across all datasets infeasible in this work. Therefore, we provide results
319   for overlapping datasets where hyperparameter tuning was performed, allowing for meaningful
320
          1Github: https://github.com/vicliv/DTE
321
          2Code: Supplementary Material Zip https://openreview.net/forum?id=_hszZbt46bT
322       3Github: https://github.com/RoelBouman/outlierdetection/tree/master
323       4Github: https://github.com/Minqi824/ADBench/tree/main
          5Github: https://github.com/yzhao062/pyod


                                                       6
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


324
      comparisons. We deliberately exclude certain methods from PyOD, such as LOCI (Papadimitriou
325   et al., 2003), due to their computational intractability 𝑂 (𝑛3 ), where 𝑛 represents the number of
326   data points. SOS (Janssens et al., 2011) was omitted due to its 𝑂 (𝑛2 ) memory requirements.
327   These methods did not show significant improvements over the algorithms included in our study.
328   Additionally, we exclude XGBod (Zhao & Hryniewicki, 2018), as it operates semi-supervised by
329   incorporating partially labeled data, which deviates from the One-Class setting that is the focus of
330   our work. Similarly, the methods presented in Pang et al. (2018) and Li et al. (2024) were not
331   considered, as they perform single sample comparisons and thus do not align with the unsupervised
332   and for the entire dataset automatic learning paradigm. The ATDAD method (Yang & Li, 2023)
333
      requires special hyperparameters for specific datasets, and due to the limited number of datasets in
      their experiments, a direct comparison with our method NCSBAD was not feasible. We provide
334
      further details in Section 6 and Appendix E.3.
335
336   Please note: For all methods used, we apply the default hyperparameters provided by the authors
337   of the original publications and used their code directly, No additional hyperparameter tuning or
338   other adjustments to the code were made. We recommend doing this additionally when using one
339
      of the baseline methods. A classification of which methods work in an inductive and which in a
      transductive setting and a further discussion of the baseline methods can be found in Appendix E.4.
340
341   Main Results Figure 2 presents the overall performance of all methods across our benchmark,
342   each dataset constrained to a maximum of 50,000 data points; we provide more details for this in
343   Appendix E.2. We show box plots of the AUC-ROC and F1-scores averaged over five different random
344   seeds (0-4) used for the undersampling and data splitting over all datasets. Our proposed method
345
      demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across the benchmark, outperforming existing techniques
      on multiple metrics. Including a validation dataset further improves our approach, establishing it as

375   different seeds, utilizing only normal samples for training. The methods are ordered according to their

      outperforms all baseline models when validation data is used. Without validation data, NCSBAD

378
      the top-performing model based on the mean values across all three evaluated metrics. Interestingly,
379   while our NCSN with validation data, NCSBADVAL, ranks first for overall mean scores, the version
380   without validation, NCSBAD, performs comparably, ranking just behind in AUC-ROC but falling
381   slightly short in F1-score and AUC-PR against the LUNAR model. The notable performance of
382   LUNAR, KPCA, and GMM—methods that are often overlooked in similar comparisons—should also
383   be highlighted here. Due to space limitations, Figure 2 only presents the summarized results. Detailed
384   results for the F1-Score, the performance on each dataset, and additional evaluation metrics proposed
385   by Campos et al. (2016) as well as rankings are reported in Appendix F. Nevertheless, a closer
386   inspection of the per-dataset results reveals a strong dependence on the specific dataset characteristics,
387
      with different algorithms outperforming others in various scenarios. This observation suggests that no
      single method universally dominates across all datasets. Consequently, we recommend practitioners
388
      evaluate multiple algorithms when confronted with a new dataset to identify the most effective
389
      approach. This highlights the importance of testing a diverse set of models to ensure optimal
390   performance for any given application. Figure 14 in Appendix F.3 compares our method’s training
391   and inference times against baseline models, alongside their computational requirements. Our
392   model leverages a straightforward neural network architecture and parallelized inference, reducing
393   inference time substantially. These results underscore the efficiency and robustness of NCSN for
394   anomaly detection. Refer to Appendix D for further comparisons with Transformer-based models.
395
396
      5   Interpretability
397
398
      In section 3, we mentioned that our proposed method possesses inherent and intuitive interpretability,
399   providing a distinct advantage in anomaly detection tasks. The anomaly score generated by our
400   model, initially aggregated as a single value for baseline experiments, can be flexibly evaluated
401   at both the aggregate and feature levels. We present a toy example demonstrating feature-level
402   anomaly localization to further illustrate this capability. We utilize the MLP2048 model and train
403   it for this demonstration using a one from the MNIST-C dataset. MNIST-C also contains images
404   with anomalies. Among other things, with an added zig-zag line. Given that the MNIST dataset
405   comprises grayscale images X ∈ R28×28 , each image is flattened into a vector x ∈ R784 to conform
406   to the model’s input requirements. Training and testing images are standardized according to training
407
      data distribution, as with all our experiments.
408   After training and inference, the result-
409   ing anomaly score—initially output as
410   a vector x ∈ R784 is reshaped into a
411   matrix X ∈ R28×28 to correspond to
412   the original image dimensions. This
413
      reshaped anomaly map is subsequently
      visualized as an image, as shown in
414
      Figure 3. The figure depicts the origi-
415
      nal training image, the test image (with      Figure 3: Interpretability of our method NCSBAD illus-
416   an added anomaly), and the difference
417
                                                    trated on MNIST-C and MNIST-C Zig-Zag datasets. The
      between the two. Additionally, the            first image displays the training image, followed by the sec-
418   anomaly score heatmap, reshaped to            ond image, highlighting the anomaly of the testing image.
419   mirror the original image structure, is       The third image presents the difference between the train-
420   plotted. As evident from Figure 5,            ing and testing images. Finally, the last image visualizes
421   the anomaly, represented by the added         the anomaly on the image, utilizing the feature-wise score
422   zig-zag line, is identified and exhibits      output of the model reshaped into a 28x28 format.
423   strong alignment with the difference
424
      image.
425   The visualization highlights the model’s natural interpretability, showcasing its ability to detect
426   outliers at the granular feature level. This approach offers a global anomaly score and facilitates fine-
427   grained insight into the specific features contributing to the anomaly, providing a more transparent
428   and interpretable anomaly detection process. While this is indeed a vision example, the flattened
429   matrix is converted to a vector format akin to tabular data, meaning that no extended spatial patterns
430
      remain in 2D and also the MLP2048 is used. The visualization is merely a toy example selected for its
      illustrative potential. Providing an intuitive example in the context of tabular data is challenging, as
431
      noted by Shenkar & Wolf (2022). Beyond purely numerical statements we offer a tangible example.


                                                          8
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


432
      The requirements for this example are using a grayscale image with a manageable dimension for
433   ensuring comparability with tabular data. This example demonstrates that our method can localized
434   anomaly detection at the feature level, enhancing its applicability for tasks requiring detailed anomaly
435   diagnostics and offering robust interpretability.
436
437
438   6   Related Work
439
440   Anomaly detection has seen considerable advances, particularly in tabular data, with several key
441   studies focusing on method comparison (Bouman et al., 2024), The most comprehensive comparison
442   of unsupervised methods, the development of new techniques (Bergman & Hoshen, 2020; Shenkar &
443
      Wolf, 2022; Livernoche et al., 2024; Thimonier et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024; Yin et al., 2024), and Zhao
      et al. (2019) or Han et al. (2022) offer detailed benchmarks on various datasets. Interestingly, high-
444
      performing GMM (Agarwal, 2007) and LUNAR (Goodge et al., 2022) are often omitted as baselines
445
      in recent works, along with KPCA (Hoffmann, 2007). For one-class classification anomaly detection,
446   non-parametric methods are popular, like the historic kernel density estimation (Parzen, 1962) or
447   the modern COPOD (Li et al., 2020). At the same time, parametric approaches include Gaussian-
448   based methods (Agarwal, 2007) and adversarial learning (Zenati et al., 2018)or classical regularized
449   classifiers, particularly kernel-based methods (Schölkopf et al., 1999), also offer an alternative to
450   density estimation approaches by enforcing tighter fits around observed data. Recent developments
451   have been driven by deep learning. Early models focused on autoencoders (Sakurada & Yairi, 2014;
452   Aggarwal & Aggarwal, 2017; Kingma, 2013; Hawkins et al., 2002) and distance-based techniques
453   (Ramaswamy et al., 2000), with later methods integrating deep learning and one-class classification
454
      (Pang et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2022). Many current methods adopt a self-supervised approach,
      leveraging task-specific loss functions to improve anomaly detection without labeled anomalies
455
      (Golan & El-Yaniv, 2018; Hendrycks et al., 2019; Li et al., 2023; Qiu et al., 2021; Schneider et al.,
456
      2022; Sohn et al., 2020). A comprehensive review is presented in the following surveys Ruff et al.
457   (2021); Pang et al. (2021); Chalapathy & Chawla (2019); Chandola et al. (2009).
458
459   Score-based Models for Anomaly Detection There are two primary approaches for learning the
460
      score of a probability density function: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (Ho et al., 2020)
      and Noise Conditional Score-based Networks (Song & Ermon, 2019). While diffusion models have
461
      been extensively applied for anomaly detection in image and video data in one-class semi-supervised
462
      settings (Tur et al., 2023; Yan et al., 2023), their use in the context of tabular data and unsupervised
463   settings was explored in (Livernoche et al., 2024). In Wolleb et al. (2022), a diffusion-based encoding
464   method followed by a classifier-guided denoising procedure was proposed, and Zhang et al. (2023a)
465   introduced the synthesis of anomaly samples for training denoising networks focused on anomaly
466   repair. AnoDDPM (Wyatt et al., 2022) trains a denoising network using diffusion noise to reconstruct
467   normal images, while Graham et al. (2023) extends this approach by combining reconstructions across
468   multiple time steps to generate anomaly scores. On the other hand, NCSNs are less explored, and
469   only closely related work exists. Shin et al. (2023) leveraged perturbation resilience from a geometric
470   perspective for industrial image anomaly detection, while Cai & Fan (2022) perturbed normal data
471
      and trained a classifier to differentiate normal from anomaly samples. NCSN-based sampling has
      been applied to time-series anomaly detection Lim et al. (2023), combining reconstruction-based,
472
      density-based, and gradient-based anomaly scores. Energy-based models closely related to NCSNs
473
      have also been employed for manifold recovery in Yoon et al. (2023).
474
475   Score-based Models for Tabular Data Unlike image datasets, where pixel values typically
476   follow Gaussian distributions, tabular data exhibits more complex, heterogeneous distributions across
477
      columns (Xu et al., 2019). This discrepancy for tabular data requires special consideration and
      suggests that score-based models that can learn complex data distributions are well-suited (Kim
478
      et al., 2022). Diffusion models have recently been adapted for this domain, such as applying DDPM
479
      to tabular data (Livernoche et al., 2024), where a modified ResNet (Gorishniy et al., 2021) architecture
480   with time embeddings is incorporated before each block. Further advancements include TabDDPM
481   (Kotelnikov et al., 2023), which utilizes a simple MLP to model the reverse diffusion process, adapted
482   from prior work on diffusion models Gorishniy et al. (2021). Kim et al. (2022) employs an MLP
483   architecture inspired by Grathwohl et al. (2018) for a score-based stochastic differential equation
484   network tailored for tabular data synthesis. Additionally, Zhang et al. (2024) integrates variational
485   autoencoder (VAE) embeddings to synthesize new tabular samples using an MLP-based network
      trained via denoising score matching.


                                                         9
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


486
      7   Conclusion
487
488
      This work introduces a new paradigm in anomaly detection in a LPUE setting by utilizing the power
489   of score-based generative models with a general framework and a fixed architecture to effectively
490   differentiate normal from anomalous behavior. By leveraging an anomaly score based on an NCSN
491   only trained with normal behavior samples as a measure of anomaly, we present a robust, scalable,
492   and interpretable approach to detecting anomalies in tabular data. Our extensive evaluations on
493   ADBench and other widely used benchmarks validate the superiority of this method, positioning it
494   as a compelling alternative to existing deep learning and classical anomaly detection techniques.
495   The improvement of our method over DDPMs warrants particular emphasis, as it addresses key
496   limitations of DDPMs while enhancing both performance and efficiency. A major advantage of our
497   approach lies in its inherent parallelizability, which is absent in DDPMs. DDPMs rely on a stepwise
498   denoising process modeled as a Markov chain, requiring numerous sequential steps and significant
499   computational time for anomaly detection in a reconstruction-based framework. In contrast, as
500   detailed in Chapter 2, NCSNs learn SDEs that approximate the gradient of the log-likelihood. This
501   enables direct anomaly scoring without the need for reconstruction and allows multiple predictions
502   to be processed in parallel, drastically reducing inference times. Our method outperforms the DDPM
503
      and DTE-NP proposed by Livernoche et al. (2024) both in terms of anomaly detection results and
      computational efficiency. Additionally, we surpass the performance of DTE-IG and DTE-C, which
504
      employ time estimation during inference to generate anomaly scores. While these demonstrate
505
      faster inference times, they fall short of achieving the superior results delivered by our approach.
506   Regarding interpretability, the methods presented in Livernoche et al. (2024) are comparable, with
507   the restriction that they require an architecture specifically adapted to image data.
508
509
      The significance of this work lies not only in its solid empirical results but also in its potential to
      address key challenges in the anomaly detection landscape. As the data manifold hypothesis (Song
510
      et al., 2020b) that data is supported on a lower-dimensional structure embedded in a high-dimensional
511
      space—becomes more widely accepted, score-based models offer a principled way to exploit this.
512   While traditional methods such as locally linear embedding and manifold Parzen windows (Vincent
513   & Bengio, 2002) aim to approximate local regions of the manifold and reconstruction-based methods
514   like denoising autoencoders (Vincent et al., 2008) attempt to map between latent and data spaces,
515   our NCSN approach directly leverages the score function to capture the structure of the manifold and
516   its deviations. This makes it particularly suited for unsupervised one-class settings, where labeled
517   data is scarce, and the goal is to detect anomalies by modeling the normal data distribution alone.
518
519   8   Limitation and Future Work
520
521   While our approach NCSBAD, like other score-based models, demands substantial computational
522   resources and time during training, it offers a distinct advantage regarding inference efficiency. Unlike
523   traditional diffusion models such as DDPMs, our framework does not rely on a sequential Markov
524   chain process for inference. This allows us to parallelize the inference step, reducing inference time
525   compared to other diffusion-based techniques.
526   Our work is a foundational contribution to establishing NCSNs as a competitive method for anomaly
527   detection in a LPUE or one-class classification setting. We have proposed a robust and generalizable
528   framework, focusing on the fundamental principles, architectural design, and parameter settings
529   necessary for effective anomaly detection using NCSNs. Future research could explore advanced
530   training strategies, such as sliced score matching (Song et al., 2020a) and maximum likelihood
531   weighting (Song et al., 2021), to optimize training efficiency and potentially enhance performance
532   further.
533
534   9   Reproducibility Statement
535
536   To ensure full transparency and reproducibility of our results, we have made the experimental code
537   available with detailed instructions in a README file, in the supplementary material zip file. Further
538   information on models, hyperparameters, datasets, baselines, and experimental procedures can be
539   found in Sections E.1, E.2, and E.3. Additionally, Section F includes an exhaustive listing of all
      results, including intermediate and partial outcomes, for further clarity and verification.

      A    Ablation, Parameters and Architecture Studies
884
885
      We perform extensive ablation studies, parameter tuning, and architectural analyses to evaluate the
886   impact of various factors on our proposed method, thereby validating our theoretical assumptions.
887   All experiments were carried out using the ADBench benchmark, employing seed 1 for consistency,
888   and we report the mean AUC-ROC for the most influential parameters. The first shown experiments
889   were conducted using the MLP2048 architecture, as presented in Section 3.
890
891
892
893
894
895
896
897
           (a) Influence of 𝜎     (b) Influence batch size    (c) Influence embed dim   (d) Influence of NUM
898
899   Figure 3: Mean AUC-ROC scores across the ADBench datasets using the MLP2048 model with
900   seed 1, evaluated under various parameter configurations. Results are presented for both settings:
901   without and using validation data.
902
903   Our first study investigates the effect of the noise standard deviation parameter 𝜎. As predicted by
904   theory, Figure 3a demonstrates our assumption that smaller 𝜎 values facilitate better learning of the
905   underlying data structure. This confirms the theoretical insights discussed in Section 3. Note this is
906   the parameter 𝜎 not 𝜎𝑡 .
907   Another critical parameter is the batch size, which, as shown in Figure Figure 3b, significantly affects
908   both convergence speed and overall model performance. The model consistently benefits from a
909   smaller batch size due to more frequent parameter updates and better adaptability to individual data
910   distributions—a batch size of 16 yields optimal results, balancing training efficiency and performance.
911   However, reducing the batch size further is not advisable as it may lead to unreasonably long training
912   times.
913   The embedding dimension, which directly influences model capacity, is another key factor, par-
914   ticularly for datasets with high feature counts. To avoid compressing the data, we set the embedding
915   dimension to be larger than the highest feature number across the benchmark. For example, since
916   the InternetAds dataset has the highest number of features (input x ∈ R1555 for InternetAds dataset),
917   we set the embedding dimension to 2048, as illustrated in Figure Figure 3c. This choice ensures
      sufficient capacity for all datasets in ADBench.


                                                             17
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


918
      Finally, we explore the effect of multiple perturbations denoted by NUM in Algorithm 2 during
919   inference. As shown in Figure Figure 3d, the random effects of perturbations stabilize after ap-
920   proximately ten repetitions, indicating that additional perturbations do not significantly improve
921   performance beyond this point.
922
      Additionally, we evaluate the impact of incorporating validation data across all experimental set-
923
      tings. As depicted in the accompanying figures, the mean AUC-ROC performance gain with vali-
924
      dation data ranges from approximately 1% in optimal settings to up to 3% in sub-optimal settings,
925   depending on the chosen hyperparameters and model configurations. This consistent enhancement
926   underscores the advantage of validation-based training in our framework and highlights its effective-
927   ness in achieving superior performance across various settings.
928
      We have carried out a comprehensive analysis of various possible architectures. As mentioned
929
      in Section 3, the only constraint is that the input dimension must match the output dimension.
930
      Consequently, we included all models from our benchmark that satisfy this criterion: VAE, the
931   tabular ResNet of DDPM, the model used in ICL, the model used in DTE-C, DiffWave (Kong
932   et al., 2020), a reduced version of DiffWave, and TabSyn (Zhang et al., 2024). DiffWave is an
933
934
935
936
937
938
939
940
941
942
943
944
945
946
947
948
949
950
      Figure 4: Comparison of various model architectures compatible with our proposed method, where
951
      the input and output dimensions must be identical. Additionally, the original performance of each
952   architecture is included as a baseline (labeled as *Org*).
953
954   architecture frequently used in the literature, based on convolutional neural networks for diffusion
955   models, primarily used to generate sound waves. TabSyn, on the other hand, is explicitly designed to
956   generate tabular data. Additionally, our proposed MLP2048 architecture, as described in Section 3,
957   was included for comparison.
958
      The results of these evaluations are presented in Figure Figure 4. We also report the original
959   benchmark results for each tested model for benchmarking purposes. The findings indicate that most
960   architectures either achieve better AUC-ROC scores with our approach or are at least comparable
961   to their original performances. Moreover, these results support our hypothesis that MLPs are
962   particularly well-suited for handling tabular data, outperforming other architectures in this context.
963   This is particularly evident in the results of the ResNet and DiffWave architectures. As we anticipated,
964   models designed for tabular data benefit more from the MLP approach, which emphasizes capturing
965   feature interactions, compared to convolutional neural networks (CNNs), which are better suited for
966   detecting spatial correlations. The insights gained from these architectural studies culminate in the
967
      framework presented in Section 3, providing further validation of our proposed methodology.
968
969   B    Algorithm
970
971   This section provides a detailed algorithmic illustration of the proposed method. Algorithm 1
      outlines the diffusion phases involved in the training process, where the model learns complex


                                                        18
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


972
       underlying data distributions. Algorithm 2 demonstrates the inference procedure for anomaly
973    detection, where the anomalies are estimated based on the learned score model. This process
974    leverages the training loss from multiple perturbed samples to identify anomalies.
975

1041   D    Comparison of Results with Transformer Models
1042
1043   Since NPT-AD (Thimonier et al., 2023) utilizes distinct hyperparameters for training on each dataset
1044   and a comprehensive search for these parameters is beyond the scope of this work, we omit NPT
1045
       in our comprehensive benchmark. Nonetheless, to provide a comparison against Transformer-based
       models, we compare our results with those reported in their study. Generally, their approach aligns
1046
       with ours regarding training setup and evaluation protocol, following the methodology outlined in
1047
       (Shenkar & Wolf, 2022). A key difference in their evaluation lies in their use of multiple 5% T-tests
1048   to calculate the mean and standard deviation rather than utilizing the entire test split across multiple
1049   random seeds as we do. Despite this variation, we believe the results are sufficiently comparable to
1050   be included here.
1051
1052       Method             Transformer           NPT-AD            NCSBAD (Ours)                 NCSBADVAL (Ours)
1053
           wine                 61.4 (6.7)         96.6 (0.5)             99.97 (0.07)                    99.98 (0.02)
1054
           Lymphography         99.5 (0.4)         99.0 (1.1)             99.97 (0.06)                    99.90 (0.13)
1055

1260   was carried out with the seeds 0-4 used in the benchmark.
