1276
1277   The preprocessed dataset (without anomalies) is then used in the training data at 50% as explained
1278   in Section 4. The remaining data is enriched with the anomalies and used as validation and test data
1279   in a ratio of 40% to 60%. If rounding is necessary, it is rounded in favor of the test data.
1280
1281   E.3    Baseline Methods
1282
1283
       Clustering Based Local Outlier Factor (CBLOF) (He et al., 2003). CBLOF calculates an outlier
       score based on cluster size and distance to large clusters. Data points in small clusters are flagged
1284
       as anomalies if far from large clusters. Weights based on cluster size are optional but disabled
1285
       by default due to potential misbehavior. By default, K-means is used for clustering instead of the
1286   original Squeezer algorithm for efficiency.
1287
1288   Isolation-based Anomaly Detection using Nearest-Neighbor Ensembles (INNE) (Bandaragoda
1289
       et al., 2018). INNE is a fast anomaly detector based on nearest neighbor ensembles, addressing
       weaknesses of isolation forests, such as detecting local anomalies and handling high-dimensional
1290
       data. It achieves linear time complexity, making it significantly faster than traditional nearest-
1291
       neighbor methods, especially for large datasets.
1292
1293   Kernel Principal Component Analysis (KPCA) (Hoffmann, 2007). KPCA extends PCA to non-
1294   linear spaces, using the reconstruction error in feature space as the anomaly score. Data is mapped
1295
       into an infinite-dimensional space, and anomalies are detected based on their distance from the
       principal subspace, serving as a novelty detection method.


                                                         24
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1296
       Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) (Latecki et al., 2007). KDE detects outliers by comparing the
1297   local density of a point to its neighbors. A robust local density estimate is calculated using a variable
1298   kernel, and the negative log probability density is used as the anomaly score.
1299
1300
       Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) (Agarwal, 2007). GMM detects anomalies by fitting a two-
       component Gaussian mixture to deviations in data over time. This approach reduces false positives
1301
       by mitigating the multiple testing problem and is scalable for real-time monitoring of large datasets.
1302
1303   Subspace Outlier Detection (SOD) (Kriegel et al., 2009). SOD aims to identify outliers in varying
1304   subspaces of high-dimensional feature spaces. For each data point, SOD examines the axis-parallel
1305   subspace defined by the point’s neighbors and measures how much it deviates from them in this
1306
       subspace.
1307   Adversarially Learned Anomaly Detection (ALAD) (Zenati et al., 2018). ALAD leverages bi-
1308   directional GANs to derive adversarially learned features. Anomalies are detected through recon-
1309   struction errors of these features. ALAD ensures data-space and latent-space cycle consistencies to
1310   stabilize GAN training, significantly improving detection performance.
1311   Copula-Based Outlier Detection (COPOD) (Hoffmann, 2007). COPOD models multivariate data
1312   distributions using copulas to estimate tail probabilities for each data point, which indicates its
1313   level of extremeness or anomaly. COPOD is parameter-free, computationally efficient, and highly
1314   interpretable. It has shown superior performance and offers a fast Python implementation for
1315   reproducibility.
1316   Empirical-Cumulative-distribution-based Outlier Detection (ECOD) (Li et al., 2022). ECOD
1317   addresses challenges in unsupervised outlier detection, such as high computational cost and limited
1318   interpretability. ECOD estimates the empirical cumulative distribution per dimension of the data
1319   and computes tail probabilities. Outlier scores are then calculated by aggregating these probabilities
1320   across dimensions. ECOD is parameter-free, easy to interpret, scalable, and efficient.
1321
       FeatureBagging (Lazarevic & Kumar, 2005). Feature Bagging for outlier detection combines results
1322   from multiple outlier detection algorithms, each applied to different randomly selected feature subsets.
1323   The outlier scores from these algorithms are aggregated to improve the overall detection quality. This
1324   approach has shown non-trivial improvements.
1325
       Histogram-based Outlier Detection (HBOS) (Goldstein & Dengel, 2012). HBOS is an efficient
1326
       algorithm that scores records in linear time. It assumes feature independence, which allows for much
1327
       faster computations than multivariate methods, albeit at the cost of reduced precision. HBOS reliably
1328   detects global outliers, performing comparably to state-of-the-art methods. However, it struggles
1329   with local outlier problems. In experiments, HBOS is up to 5 times faster than clustering-based
1330   algorithms and 7 times faster than nearest-neighbor-based methods.
1331
       Isolation Forest (iForest) (Liu et al., 2008). Isolation Forest introduces a fundamentally different
1332
       approach by isolating anomalies rather than profiling normal points. Isolation enables the method to
1333
       leverage sub-sampling extensively, making the algorithm highly scalable with linear time complexity,
1334   low memory requirements, and favorable performance on large datasets.
1335
1336   k-th Nearest Neighbor (kNN) (Ramaswamy et al., 2000). This method identifies distance-based
1337
       outliers by ranking each point based on its distance to its k-th nearest neighbor. The top-n points
       in this ranking are declared outliers. The algorithm uses classical nested-loop join and index join
1338
       techniques but also introduces a highly efficient partition-based algorithm that prunes partitions that
1339
       cannot contain outliers.
1340
1341   Lightweight Online Detector of Anomalies (LODA) (Pevnỳ, 2016). LODA demonstrates that an
1342   ensemble of weak detectors can yield a robust anomaly detector, performing as well as or better than
1343
       state-of-the-art methods. LODA is particularly useful for real-time processing of large datasets or
       in scenarios with concept drift, where the detector must update online. It operates efficiently even
1344
       with missing variables and can highlight features responsible for anomalies, making it practical for
1345
       sensor-outage domains.
1346
1347   Local Outlier Factor (LOF) (Breunig et al., 2000). The LOF algorithm assigns each object a score
1348   of being an outlier based on how isolated it is relative to its neighborhood. This local approach
1349
       detects meaningful outliers that existing binary outlier methods cannot capture. LOF is practical and
       effective at identifying meaningful outliers that existing approaches would miss.


                                                          25
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1350
       Minimum Covariance Determinant (MCD) (Fauconnier & Haesbroeck, 2009). The MCD co-
1351   variance estimator is designed for Gaussian-distributed data but can also be applied to data from an
1352   unimodal, symmetric distribution. It is unsuitable for multi-modal data, where the algorithm will
1353   likely fail. For multi-modal datasets, projection pursuit methods are recommended. MCD fits a
1354   minimum covariance determinant model and then computes the Mahalanobis distance as the outlier
1355   score.
1356
       One-Class Support Vector Machine (OCSVM) (Schölkopf et al., 1999). The OCSVM estimates a
1357   function 𝑓 positive on a subset 𝑆 of the data and negative on its complement. The function is based
1358   on a kernel expansion from a subset of training data and is regularized by controlling the weight
1359   vector in the associated feature space. OCSVM extends support vector algorithms to the case of
1360   unlabeled data.
1361
       Principal Component Analysis (PCA) (Shyu et al., 2003). This approach utilizes a robust PCA
1362
       classifier for intrusion detection in unsupervised settings. Anomalies are treated as outliers, and
1363   the intrusion predictive model is built using the major and minor principal components of normal
1364   data instances. The distance in principal component space between the anomalous and normal data
1365   determines the anomaly score.
1366
       Extended Isolation Forest (EIF) (Hariri et al., 2019). EIF addresses issues with anomaly score
1367
       assignment in the original Isolation Forest. The problem is demonstrated using heat maps, where
1368
       artifacts in anomaly scores result from binary tree branching operations. EIF resolves this by
1369   introducing two methods: (1) random data transformation before tree creation, which averages out
1370   bias, and (2) slicing the data using hyperplanes with random slopes, mitigating the artifacts. This
1371   approach improves the algorithm’s robustness, as shown by reduced score variance for data points
1372   along constant-level sets.
1373
       Ensemble LOF: (Breunig et al., 2000; Bouman et al., 2024). This is an ensemble version of the
1374
       LOF algorithm.
1375
1376   GEN2OUT (Lee et al., 2021). GEN2OUT handles both point and group anomalies in one framework,
1377   ranking anomalies by suspiciousness. GEN2OUT’s main features include a principled anomaly
1378
       scoring that adheres to detection axioms, a dual capability to detect and rank generalized anomalies,
       and scalability, as it runs in linear time concerning input size.
1379
1380   DynamicHOBS (Goldstein & Dengel, 2012; Bouman et al., 2024). A newer implementation of the
1381   HBOS algorithm.
1382   Connectivity-based Outlier Factor (COF) (Hoffmann, 2007). COF improves the effectiveness of
1383   LOF when a pattern has a neighborhood density similar to that of an outlier. It is theoretical and
1384   empirical, providing more effective outlier detection.
1385
       Angle-Based Outlier Detection (ABOD) (Tang et al., 2002). ABOD and its variants assess the
1386
       angle variance between the difference vectors of a point and the other points. A significant advantage
1387
       of ABOD is that it does not rely on any parameter selection that could influence the ranking quality.
1388   ABOD performs exceptionally well on high-dimensional data.
1389
1390   Linear Method for Deviation Detection (LMDD) (Kriegel et al., 2008). LMDD approaches the
1391
       problem from within the data, using implicit redundancy. It formalizes the problem and proposes a
       linear algorithm to detect deviations. The solution mimics human behavior. After observing a series
1392
       of similar data, a deviating element is recognized as an exception.
1393
1394   Outlier Detection using Indegree Number (ODIN) (Hautamaki et al., 2004). The ODIN algorithm
1395   utilizes a k-nearest neighbor graph to detect outliers. It improves on existing kNN distance-based
1396   methods. ODIN shows competitive results on synthetic data and outperforms other methods on real
1397
       datasets with smaller observation counts.
1398   Cook’s Distance (CD) (Cook, 1977). CD identifies points negatively affecting a regression model.
1399   It combines each observation’s leverage and residual values. Higher leverage and residual values
1400   correspond to higher Cook’s Distance. This unsupervised method requires at least two features (for
1401   𝑋) to calculate each data point’s mean Cook’s Distance.
1402   Quasi-Monte Carlo Discrepancy (QMCD) (Fang & Ma, 2001). The Wrap-around QMCD is a
1403   uniformity criterion used to evaluate the space-filling properties of samples within a hypercube. It


                                                        26
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1404
       measures the difference between the continuous uniform distribution on a hypercube and the discrete
1405   uniform distribution of sample points. Lower discrepancy values indicate better parameter space
1406   coverage. This kernel-based method assigns higher discrepancy scores to outlier candidates than the
1407   other samples.
1408
       Sampling (Sugiyama & Borgwardt, 2013). A simple sampling-based scheme is an alternative to
1409
       distance-based approaches like k-nearest neighbor methods that are widely used in outlier detection
1410
       as they avoid the need to model underlying probability distributions, which is particularly challenging
1411   in high-dimensional data and outperforms many techniques in both efficiency and effectiveness.
1412
1413   Learnable Unified Neighbourhood-based Anomaly Ranking (LUNAR) (Goodge et al., 2022).
1414
       LUNAR unifies local outlier methods by demonstrating that they are special cases of the more
       general message-passing framework used in graph neural networks. This enables the introduction
1415
       of learnability into local outlier methods, allowing for greater flexibility and expressivity. LUNAR
1416
       uses a graph neural network-based anomaly detection technique that learns to leverage information
1417   from each node’s nearest neighbors in a trainable manner.
1418
1419   Single-Objective Generative Adversarial Active Learning (SOGAAL) / Multiple Generators
1420
       with different Objectives (MO-GAAL) (Liu et al., 2019). The SOGAAL method for outlier
       detection directly generates informative potential outliers based on the mini-max game between a
1421
       generator and a discriminator. To mitigate the mode-collapsing problem, SOGAAL must stop training
1422
       at the correct point. MOGAALL is an extension to provide a reasonable reference distribution across
1423   the entire dataset.
1424
1425   Autoencoder (AE) (Aggarwal & Aggarwal, 2017; Ramaswamy et al., 2000). The AE method is
1426
       based on reconstruction. In this context, an AE is trained to minimize reconstruction errors, which
       are used to measure anomaly detection.
1427
1428   Deep Support Vector Data Description (DeepSVDD) (Ruff et al., 2018). DeepSVDD is built
1429   on kernel-based SVDD and minimum volume estimation by finding the smallest data-enclosing
1430   hypersphere. In Deep SVDD, we learn helpful feature representations of the data and the one-class
1431   classification objective. A neural network is trained jointly to map the data into a hypersphere of
1432
       minimum volume, capturing useful patterns for outlier detection.
1433   Deep Autoencoding Gaussian Mixture Model (DAGMM) (Zong et al., 2018). DAGMM is a deep
1434   autoencoder that produces a low-dimensional representation and reconstruction error for each input.
1435   This output is further fed into a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM). Instead of using a two-stage training
1436   process and the standard Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm, DAGMM jointly optimizes the
1437   deep autoencoder and the mixture model parameters in an end-to-end fashion, leveraging a separate
1438
       estimation network to aid in parameter learning.
1439   Deep Robust One Class Classification (DROCC) (Goyal et al., 2020). DROCC is a method that
1440   applies to standard domains without requiring side information and remains robust to representa-
1441   tion collapse. The method assumes that points from the class of interest lie on a well-sampled,
1442   locally linear, low-dimensional manifold. DROCC builds on this assumption to better capture class
1443   structures.
1444   GOAD (Bergman & Hoshen, 2020). GOAD is a method designed for generalization assumptions.
1445   GOAD extends the applicability of transformation-based anomaly detection methods to non-image
1446   data through random affine transformations.
1447
       Internal Contrastive Learning (ICL) (Shenkar & Wolf, 2022). ICL captures the internal structure
1448   of the single training class by learning mappings that maximize the mutual information between each
1449   sample and its masked-out portions. The mappings are optimized using contrastive loss, computed
1450   one sample at a time. During inference, ICL scores a test sample by determining whether the
1451   mappings result in small contrastive loss using the masked parts of the sample.
1452
       PlanarFlow (Rezende & Mohamed, 2015). Normalizing flows transform a simple initial density into
1453
       a more complex one by applying a sequence of invertible transformations. This method unifies the
1454   finite and infinitesimal flow approaches, allowing for richer posterior approximations. PlanarFlow
1455   combines theoretically sound posterior matching with scalable variational inference.
1456
1457
       Variational Autoencoder (VAE) (Kingma, 2013; Zhou et al., 2020). VAE trained on normal data is
       expected to reconstruct normal inputs accurately, while anomalies are detected through deviations.


                                                         27
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1458
       GANomaly (Akcay et al., 2019). GANomaly is a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)-based
1459   method for anomaly detection, using the reconstruction error of input instances as the anomaly score.
1460   Tabular data is modified with dense layers using tanh activation functions. The encoder-decoder-
1461   encoder structure of GANomaly is set to have a hidden size equal to half the input dimension, which
1462   allows for more suitable evaluation on tabular datasets.
1463
       Scale Learning-based Anomaly Detection (SLAD) (Xu et al., 2023b). The SLAD considers the
1464
       relationship between the dimensionality of the original sub-vectors and their transformed representa-
1465   tions. These scales serve as labels for converted representations, providing abundant labeled data for
1466   neural network training. SLAD learns to align the scale distribution, enabling it to model inherent
1467   regularities in the data.
1468
       Deep Isolation Forest (DIF) (Xu et al., 2023a). DIF is a representation scheme leveraging casually
1469
       initialized neural networks to map raw data into random ensemble representations. Subsequently,
1470
       random axis-parallel cuts are applied within these representations to partition the data. The synergy
1471   between random representations and partition-based isolation gives a more efficient separation of
1472   normal instances from anomalous ones.
1473
1474
       Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs) (Livernoche et al., 2024). DDPMs utilize
       the diffusion process to implicitly learn the score function of the underlying data distribution. By
1475
       simulating the reverse diffusion process, DDPMs reconstruct input samples and use the reconstruction
1476
       distance as a metric to identify anomalies. It provides a mechanism for uncovering abnormal patterns
1477   in the data, leveraging the probabilistic framework to model intricate data distributions.
1478
1479   Diffusion Time Estimation (DTE) (Livernoche et al., 2024). DTE addresses anomaly detection by
1480
       estimating the distribution of diffusion times for a given input, utilizing either the mode or mean
       of this distribution as the anomaly score. The analytical form for this density enhances inference
1481
       efficiency through a deep neural network. The DDPM-based anomaly detection can be viewed as
1482
       a distance estimation between an input and the denoised reconstruction. Given noisy inputs, the
1483   distribution of diffusion times follows an inverse Gamma (DTE-IG) distribution. This insight forms
1484   the foundation of the non-parametric (DTE-NP) method, which accurately predicts diffusion time
1485   and produces anomaly rankings comparable to kNN. Furthermore, a categorical (DTE-C) approach
1486   leverages a deep neural network for improved generalization and fast inference.
1487
       Three-Way Neighborhood Characteristic Regions Outlier Detection (3WNCROD) (Zhang et al.,
1488
       2023b). 3WNCROD is an outlier detection approach that advances three-way neighborhood charac-
1489   teristic regions and corresponding fusion measurements. Building upon neighborhood rough sets,
1490   the method leverages three-way decisions to define neighborhood structures across model bound-
1491   aries, inner regions, and characteristic regions. These structures are then used to drive information
1492   fusion and weight measurement across features and the development of a multiple neighborhood
1493   outlier factor. The method integrates various sources of neighborhood information to enhance the
1494   robustness and accuracy of outlier detection.
1495   Masked Correlation Modeling (MCM) (Yin et al., 2024). MCM captures intrinsic correlations
1496   between features in the training set, with deviations from these correlations indicating potential
1497   anomalies. To generate multiple, diverse correlations, a masking strategy that learns to produce
1498   several masks is guided by a diversity loss that minimizes similarity between the masks. The model
1499   captures various feature correlations, increasing its robustness in detecting anomalous data points.
1500   Individual features and the relationships between them provide interpretability.
1501   Transformer / Non-Parametric Transformers (NPT) (Thimonier et al., 2023). NPT is a model
1502   initially designed for supervised tasks to capture dependencies between features and samples. In a
1503   reconstruction-based framework, the NPT reconstructs masked features of normal samples, leverag-
1504   ing the entire training dataset during inference in a non-parametric manner. The model’s ability to
1505   accurately reconstruct masked features is then used to generate an anomaly score. For comparison,
1506   a baseline Transformer under similar conditions is stated.
1507
1508   E.4   Discussion of the Baseline Methods
1509
1510   The baseline methods, as outlined in Chapter X, including their hyperparameters, were adopted
1511   exactly as implemented in the original code of their respective publications, as well as in the PyOD
       and ADBench libraries. This approach may introduce potential biases in the overall results. For


                                                        28
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1512
       instance, in the case of the VAE model from the ADBench library, a sigmoid function is used as
1513   the output activation function. We attribute this choice to the similarity between anomaly detection
1514   and binary classification, which scales the output to a probability. However, to avoid restricting the
1515   model’s learned distribution, other activation functions, such as a linear activation function, could
1516   also be considered. This serves as an example of potential optimizations that could be applied to
1517   the baseline methods and is representative of other similar opportunities. Consequently, it cannot be
1518   ruled out that other models may also possess untapped optimization potential.
1519   We strongly recommend optimizing baseline methods for the specific task at hand when applying
1520   them in practical scenarios. This recommendation applies to all baseline methods and encompasses
1521   all possible hyperparameters, including model architectures.
1522
       Furthermore, it should be noted that while the models from the ADBench library, as well as the deep
1523
       learning baseline models, are suitable for an inductive setting in line with the LUPE or one-class
1524
       classification framework described in this work, some models from PyOD or Bouman et al. (2024)
1525   operate exclusively in a transductive manner. This means that these models do not have access to the
1526   training data and make predictions solely based on the test data, which is not strictly in line with the
1527   one-class classification paradigm. This is particularly true for some purely mathematical models that
1528   do not involve training in the conventional sense. To avoid limiting the benchmark and to maintain
1529   its generalizability, these models were nevertheless included.
1530
       To assist in selecting appropriate models, Table 6 provides an overview distinguishing the inductive
1531   and transductive models and how they are included in the benchmark.
1532
1533
                               Model                     Inductive     Transductive
1534

1587   E.5   Runtime
1588
1589   The experiments on the first five seeds (0-4) of all 136 sub-datasets and 49 baseline methods, as well
1590   as one run each with score model with and without validation data, were performed on a server with
1591   2x AMD EPYC™ GENOA 9654 processors, 2.40-3.70 GHz with 1.5TB RAM and 4x NVIDIA L40
1592
       GPUs with 48 GB GDDR6 VRAM on one GPU each. All benchmark results lasted a total of 702
       GPU hours (stated over all methods. Note not all methods run on GPU).

1595   F     Additional Results
1596
1597   We present the complete results corresponding to the AUC-ROC and area under the precision-recall
1598   curve (AUC-PR) box plots in Section 4, detailed in Tables 9-16 and Tabels 23-32. We report
1599   all results from the 121 sub-datasets of the ADBench benchmark and the 15 additional datasets.
1600   Moreover, performance metrics, including the F1-Score, are provided in Tables 17-24, with the
1601
       corresponding box plots shown in Figure 13. Furthermore, Figure 14 displays plots of the mean
       training and inference times. A particular emphasis is placed on improving inference performance
1602
       compared to DDPM. Note that the y-axis is plotted on a logarithmic scale. Exact numerical values for
1603
       the box polts can be found in Table 8. All results are averaged over five random seeds, and standard
1604   deviations are reported in parentheses for each dataset in our benchmark, ensuring a comprehensive
1605   and reliable performance comparison.
1606
1607   F.1   F1-Score, Adjusted F1-Score and Adjusted AUC-PR
1608
1609   Campos et al. (2016) proposed adjusted metrics for AUC-PR and precision at n to account for biases
1610   in the different anomaly proportions of the test data. They defined: For a dataset 𝐷𝐵 of size 𝑁,
1611   consisting of outliers 𝑂 ⊂ 𝐷𝐵 and inliers 𝐼 ⊂ 𝐷𝐵 (𝐷𝐵 = 𝑂 ∪ 𝐼), 𝑃@𝑛 is formalized as:
1612
1613                                              |{𝑜 ∈ 𝑂 | rank(𝑜) ≤ 𝑛}|
                                         𝑃@𝑛 =                                                            (6)
1614                                                          𝑛
1615
1616
       With the number of outliers 𝑛 and 𝑛 = |𝑂|. Since in this work, following Bergman & Hoshen (2020),
       the decision threshold is chosen according to the number of actually existing anomalies, in this case
1617
       the 𝑃@𝑛 corresponds to the F1-score.
1618
1619
